brief: GAP/WHY: persistent AF patients undergoing catheter ablation thought to face worse outcomes (more comorbidities, more advanced arrhythmia substrate); possible barriers to ablation access vs paroxysmal AF; unclear whether persistent vs paroxysmal AF independently drives worse long-term outcomes after ablation. DESIGN/SETTING: population-based retrospective cohort using administrative data; Ontario, Canada; de novo AF catheter ablations April 2012-March 2022. POPULATION: 10,788 patients undergoing de novo AF ablation; persistent AF = 25% of cohort, paroxysmal AF = 75%. COMPARISON: persistent AF vs paroxysmal AF; inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW) to create balanced cohorts; Cox proportional hazards models estimating effect of persistent vs paroxysmal. PRIMARY OUTCOME: composite of all-cause mortality + all-cause hospitalization. BALANCE IN WEIGHTED COHORT: similar age (standardized difference 0.027), female sex (SD 0.018), Charlson comorbidity score 0.5% in both groups (SD 0.018). RESULTS (paroxysmal vs persistent, composite outcome): 30 days 5.5% vs 6.3% (HR 1.15, 95% CI 0.94-1.40, P=0.168); 1 year 19.8% vs 19.7% (HR 1.00, 95% CI 0.90-1.11, P=0.971); 3 years 34.1% vs 35.4% (HR 1.05, 95% CI 0.97-1.13, P=0.269). No increased risk of individual components (mortality, hospitalization) at 30 days, 1 year, or 3 years. TAKEAWAY: long-term all-cause mortality + hospitalization risk after ablation similar for persistent vs paroxysmal AF at 30 days, 1 year, 3 years; adverse impact of persistent AF on long-term outcomes (e.g. all-cause mortality) primarily attributable to comorbid conditions rather than AF type itself.
